question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2025

Code: 55/4/2 (set-2)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 31 printed pages.
  2. Please check that this question paper contains 33 questions.
  3. Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  4. Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book at the given place before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

PHYSICS (Theory)

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:70

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 33 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Question paper is divided into FIVE sections - Sections A, B, C, D and E.
  3. In Section A: Question numbers 1 to 16 are Multiple Choice (MCQ) type questions. Each question carries 1 mark.
  4. In Section B: Question numbers 17 to 21 are Very Short Answer (VSA) type questions. Each question carries 2 marks.
  5. In Section C: Question numbers 22 to 28 are Short Answer (SA) type questions. Each question carries 3 marks.
  6. In Section D: Question numbers 29 & 30 are Case Study-Based questions. Each question carries 4 marks.
  7. In Section E: Question numbers 31 to 33 are Long Answer (LA) type questions. Each question carries 5 marks.
  8. There is no overall choice given in the question paper. However, an internal choice has been provided in few questions in all the Sections except Section A.
  9. Kindly note that there is a separate question paper for Visually Impaired candidates.
  10. Use of calculators is NOT allowed.
    You may use the following values of physical constants wherever necessary:
    {tex}{c}=3 \times 10^8 {~m} / {s}{/tex}
    {tex}{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} {Js}{/tex}
    {tex}{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} {C}{/tex}
    {tex}\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} {~T} {~m} {~A}^{-1}{/tex}
    {tex}\varepsilon_0=8.854 \times 10^{-12} {C}^2 {~N}^{-1} {~m}^{-2}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}=9 \times 10^9 {~N} {~m}^2 {C}^{-2}{/tex}
    Mass of electron {tex}\left({m}_{{e}}\right)=9.1 \times 10^{-31} {~kg}{/tex}.
    Mass of neutron {tex}=1.675 \times 10^{-27} {~kg}{/tex}.
    Mass of proton {tex}=1.673 \times 10^{-27} {~kg}{/tex}.
    Avogadro's number {tex}=6.023 \times 10^{23}{/tex} per gram mole
    Boltzmann's constant {tex}=1.38 \times 10^{-23} {JK}^{-1}{/tex}

SECTION A
1.

An electric dipole of dipole moment {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-12} {Cm}{/tex} lies along x-axis. An electric field of magnitude {tex}2.0 \times 10^{4} {NC}^{-1}{/tex} is switched on at an instant in the region. The unit vector along the electric field is {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \hat{{i}}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{{j}}{/tex}. The magnitude of the torque acting on the dipole at that instant is:

a. {tex}0.5 \times 10^{-6} {Nm}{/tex}
b. {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-8} {Nm}{/tex}
c. {tex}2.0 \times 10^{-8} {Nm}{/tex}
d. {tex}4.0 \times 10^{-8} {Nm}{/tex}
1
2.

When the switch of the circuit is turned on, the filament of the bulb glows instantaneously because:

a. the electrons coming from the power source move fast through the initially empty filament.
b. the filament may be old having low resistance.
c. electric field is established instantaneously across the filament which pushes the electrons.
d. free electrons in the filament travel with the speed of light.
1
3.

A particle with charge {tex}q{/tex} moving with velocity {tex}\vec{v}=v_{0} \hat{i}{/tex} enters a region with magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}=B_{1} \hat{j}+B_{2} \hat{k}{/tex}. The magnitude of force experienced by the particle is:

a. {tex}{qv}_{0}\left({~B}_{1}+{B}_{2}\right){/tex}
b. {tex}q \sqrt{v_{0}\left(B_{1}+B_{2}\right)}{/tex}
c. {tex}{qv}_{0} \sqrt{\left({~B}_{1}^{2}+{B}_{2}^{2}\right)}{/tex}
d. {tex}q \sqrt{{v}_{0}\left({~B}_{1}^{2}+{B}_{2}^{2}\right)}{/tex}
1
4.

A bar magnet is initially at right angles to a uniform magnetic field. The magnet is rotated till the torque acting on it becomes one-half of its initial value. The angle through which the bar magnet is rotated is:

a. {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}
b. {tex}45^{\circ}{/tex}
c. {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}
d. {tex}75^{\circ}{/tex}
1
5.

The materials having negative magnetic susceptibility are:

a. diamagnetic
b. paramagnetic
c. ferromagnetic
d. non-magnetic
1
6.

When current in a coil changes at a steady rate from 8 A to 6 A in 4 ms, an emf of 1.5 V is induced in it. The value of self-inductance of the coil is:

a. 6 mH
b. 12 mH
c. 3 mH
d. 9 mH
1
7.

The electric field in space between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor (each of area {tex}2.5 \times 10^{-3} {~m}^{2}{/tex}) is changing at the rate of {tex}4 \times 10^{6} {Vm}^{-1} {~s}^{-1}{/tex}. The displacement current between the plates of the capacitor is:

a. {tex}1.8 \times 10^{-5} {~A}{/tex}
b. {tex}3.47 \times 10^{-6} {~A}{/tex}
c. {tex}8.85 \times 10^{-8} {~A}{/tex}
d. {tex}6.32 \times 10^{-4} {~A}{/tex}
1
8.

A long straight wire is held vertically and carries a steady current in upward direction. The shape of magnetic field lines produced by the current-carrying wire are:

a. horizontal straight lines directed radially out from the wire.
b. straight lines parallel to the current-carrying wire.
c. concentric horizonal circles around the wire.
d. coaxial helixes around the wire.
1
9.

Which of the following is an electrical conductor at room temperature?

a. Sn
b. Mica
c. Si
d. C
1
10.

The magnification produced by a spherical mirror is {tex}-2 \cdot 0{/tex}. The mirror used and the nature of the image formed will be

a. Convex and virtual
b. Concave and real
c. Concave and virtual
d. Convex and real
1
11.

Choose the correct statement:

a. Photons of light show diffraction whereas electrons do not show diffraction.
b. Electrons have momentum whereas photons do not have momentum.
c. Photons of light and electrons both exhibit dual nature.
d. All electromagnetic radiations do not have photons.
1
12.

A beam of red light and a beam of blue light have equal intensities. Which of the following statements is true?

a. The blue beam has more number of photons than the red beam.
b. The red beam has more number of photons than the blue beam.
c. Wavelength of red light is lesser than wavelength of blue light.
d. The blue light beam has lesser energy per photon than that in the red light beam.
1

Questions number 13 to 16 are Assertion (A) and Reason (R) type questions. Two statements are given one - labelled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason (R). Select the correct answer from the codes (A), (B), (C) and (D) as given below.

  1. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  2. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  3. Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
  4. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are false.
13.

Assertion (A): For monochromatic incident radiation, the emitted photoelectrons from a given metal have speed ranging from zero to a certain maximum value.
Reason (R): Each metal has a definite work function.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
14.

Assertion (A): In double slit experiment if one slit is closed, diffraction pattern due to the other slit will appear on the screen.
Reason (R): For interference, at least two waves are required.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
15.

Assertion (A): A series LCR circuit behaves as a pure resistive circuit at resonance.
Reason (R): At resonance, {tex}{X}_{{L}}={X}_{{C}}{/tex} gives {tex}\omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{{LC}}}{/tex}.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
16.

Assertion (A): n-type semiconductor is not negatively charged.
Reason (R): Neutral pentavalent impurity atom doped in intrinsic semiconductor (neutral) donates its fifth unpaired electron to the crystal lattice and becomes a positive donor.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1

SECTION B
17.

In an intrinsic semiconductor, carrier's concentration is {tex}5 \times 10^{8} {~m}^{-3}{/tex}. On doping with impurity atoms, the hole concentration becomes {tex}8 \times 10^{12} {~m}^{-3}{/tex}.

  1. Identify (i) the type of dopant and (ii) the extrinsic semiconductor so formed.
  2. Calculate the electron concentration in the extrinsic semiconductor.
 2
18.

In a double slit experiment, the two slits are 1.5 mm apart. The slits are illuminated by a mixture of lights of wavelengths of 600 nm and 400 nm and the interference pattern is observed on a screen 1.5 m away from the slits. Find the minimum distance of the point from the central maximum at which bright fringes of the interference patterns of the two wavelengths coincide.

 2
19.

Find the focal length of plano-convex lens of refractive index 1.5 and radius of curvature 10 cm when it is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.25.

 2
20.

Find the ratio of minimum to maximum wavelength of radiations emitted when electron jumps from higher energy state into ground state of hydrogen atom.

 2
21.(i)

In the given figure, three identical bulbs {tex}{P}, {Q}{/tex} and S are connected to a battery.

  1. Compare the brightness of bulbs P and Q with that of bulb S when key K is closed.
  2. Compare the brightness of the bulbs S and Q when the key K is opened.
    Justify your answer in both cases.
 2

OR
(ii)

Two cells of emf 10 V each, two resistors of {tex}20 \Omega{/tex} and {tex}10 \Omega{/tex} and a bulb B of {tex}10 \Omega{/tex} resistance are connected together as shown in the figure. Find the current that flows through the bulb.

 2

SECTION C
22.

Explain the following observations using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

  1. Photoelectric emission does not occur from a surface when the frequency of the light incident on it is less than a certain minimum value.
  2. It is the frequency, and not the intensity of the incident light which affects the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons.
  3. The cut-off voltage ({tex}{V}_{{o}}{/tex}) versus frequency ({tex}v{/tex}) of the incident light curve is a straight line with a slope {tex}\frac{{h}}{{e}}{/tex}.
 3
23.
  1. A charged particle {tex}q{/tex} moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} is subjected to a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} acting perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow{{v}}{/tex}. If a uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is also set up in the region along the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex}, describe the path followed by the particle and draw its shape.
  2. How will the magnetic field inside a long solenoid be affected when:
    1. the radius of the turns of the solenoid is increased,
    2. the length of solenoid as well as the total number of its turns are doubled?
 3
24.
  1. Differentiate between magnetic flux through an area and magnetic field at a point.
  2. A bar magnet is held with its length along the axis of a closed coil. Initially the south pole of the magnet faces the coil. If the magnet is moved towards the coil, explain how a current is induced in the coil and in what direction.
 3
25.
  1. State any three characteristics of electromagnetic waves.
  2. Briefly explain how and where the displacement current exists during the charging of a capacitor.
 3
26.
  1. Define 'wavefront' of a light wave. A plane wavefront is refracted from a convex lens. Draw the shape of the refracted wavefront.
  2. A plane wave travelling in a medium is incident on a plane surface separating this medium from a rarer medium. Draw a diagram to show refraction of the wave. Hence, verify Snell's law.
 3
27.
  1. What are majority and minority charge carriers of p-type and n-type semiconductors?
  2. Explain briefly the formation of diffusion current and drift current in a p-n junction diode.
 3
28.(i)
  1. Derive an expression for the resistivity of a conductor in terms of number density of free electrons and relaxation time.
  2. The figure shows the plot of current through a cross-section of wire over two different time intervals. Compare the charges ({tex}{Q}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{Q}_{2}{/tex}) that pass through the cross-section during these time intervals.
 3

OR
(ii)
  1. A battery of emf E and internal resistance r is connected to a variable external resistance {tex}R{/tex}.
    1. Obtain the expression for current I in the circuit and the value of maximum current the battery can supply.
    2. Obtain the terminal voltage V across the battery and its maximum possible value.
  2. The above battery sends a current {tex}{I}_{1}{/tex} when {tex}{R}={R}_{1}{/tex} and a current {tex}I_{2}{/tex} when {tex}R=R_{2}{/tex}. Obtain the internal resistance of the battery in terms of {tex}{I}_{1}, {I}_{2}, {R}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{2}{/tex}.
 3

SECTION D

Questions number 29 and 30 are Case Study-based questions. Read the following paragraphs and answer the questions that follow.
29.

Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
A hydrogen atom consists of an electron revolving in a circular orbit of radius {tex}r{/tex} with certain velocity {tex}v{/tex} around a proton located at the nucleus of the atom. The electrostatic force of attraction between the revolving electron and the proton provides the requisite centripetal force to keep it in the orbit. According to Bohr's model, an electron can revolve only in certain stable orbits. The angular momentum of the electron in these orbits is some integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}, where {tex}h{/tex} is the Planck's constant. Further, when an electron makes a transition from one orbit of higher energy to that of lower energy, a photon is emitted having energy equal to the difference between energies of the initial and final states. Assuming the mass and charge of an electron as {tex}m{/tex} and e respectively, answer the following questions.

  1. The expression for the speed of electron v in terms of radius of the orbit ({tex}r{/tex}) and physical constant ({tex}K=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{/tex}) is:
    1. {tex}\frac{{Ke}^2}{{mr}}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{{Ke}^2}{{mr}^2}{/tex}
    3. {tex}\sqrt{\frac{{Ke}^2}{{mr}}}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\sqrt{\frac{{Ke}^2}{{mr}^2}}{/tex}
  2. The total energy of the atom in terms of r and physical constant K is:
    1. {tex}\frac{{Ke}^2}{{r}}{/tex}
    2. {tex}-\frac{{Ke}^2}{2 {r}}{/tex}
    3. {tex} \frac{{Ke}^2}{2 {r}}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\frac{3}{2} \frac{{Ke}^2}{{r}}{/tex}
  3. A photon of wavelength 500 nm is emitted when an electron makes a transition from one state to the other state in an atom. The change in the total energy of the electron and change in its kinetic energy in eV as per Bohr's model, respectively will be:
    1. {tex}2.48,-2.48{/tex}
    2. {tex}-1.24,1.24{/tex}
    3. {tex}-2 \cdot 48,2 \cdot 48{/tex}
    4. {tex}1 \cdot 24,-1 \cdot 24{/tex}
    1. In Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, the frequency of revolution of electron in its {tex}{n}^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit is proportional to:
      1. n
      2. {tex}\frac{1}{n}{/tex}
      3. {tex}\frac{1}{{n}^2}{/tex}
      4. {tex}\frac{1}{{n}^3}{/tex}

        OR

    2. An electron makes a transition from -3.4 eV state to the ground state in hydrogen atom. Its radius of orbit changes by: (radius of orbit of electron in ground state {tex}=0.53 \overset o A{/tex})
      1. {tex}0.53 \overset o A{/tex}
      2. {tex}1.06 \overset o A{/tex}
      3. {tex}1.59 \overset o A{/tex}
      4. {tex}2 \cdot 12 \overset o A{/tex}
 4
30.

Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
A parallel plate capacitor consists of two conducting plates kept generally parallel to each other at a distance. When the capacitor is charged, the charge resides on the inner surfaces of the plates and an electric field is set up between them. Thus, electrostatic energy is stored in the capacitor. The figure shows three large square metallic plates, each of side '{tex}L{/tex}' held parallel and equidistant from each other. The space between {tex}P_1{/tex} and {tex}P_2{/tex} and {tex}P_2{/tex} and {tex}P_3{/tex} is completely filled with mica sheets of dielectric constant '{tex}K{/tex}'. The plate {tex}P_2{/tex} is connected to point {tex}A{/tex} and other plates {tex}P_1{/tex} and {tex}P_3{/tex} are connected to point B. Point A is maintained at a positive potential with respect to point B and the potential difference between A and B is V.

  1. The capacitance of the system between A and B will be:
    1. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 K^2}{d}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 K L^2}{2 d}{/tex}
    3. {tex} \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 K^2}{{~d}}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 {Kd}}{{L}^2}{/tex}
  2. The charge on plate {tex}P_1{/tex} is:
    1. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 {VKL}^2}{2 {~d}}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 V K L^2}{d}{/tex}
    3. {tex} \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 V K L^2}{{~d}}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 {VKL}^2}{4 {~d}}{/tex}
  3. The electric field in the region between {tex}P_1{/tex} and {tex}P_2{/tex} is:
    1. {tex}\frac{{V}}{{d}}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{2 {~V}}{{~d}}{/tex}
    3. {tex}\frac{{V}}{2 {~d}}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\frac{{d}}{{V}}{/tex}
    1. The separation between the plates of same area {tex}\left(L^2\right){/tex} of a parallel plate air capacitor having capacitance equal to that of this system, will be:
      1. {tex}\frac{{d}}{{K}}{/tex}
      2. {tex}\frac{2 {~d}}{{~K}}{/tex}
      3. {tex}\frac{{d}}{2 {~K}}{/tex}
      4. {tex}\frac{{d}}{4 {~K}}{/tex}

        OR

    2. If the source of potential difference applied between A and B is removed, and then A and B are connected by a conducting wire, the net charge on the system will be:
      1. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 {VKL}^2}{4 {~d}}{/tex}
      2. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 {VKL}^2}{2 {~d}}{/tex}
      3. {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_0 {VKL}^2}{{~d}}{/tex}
      4. Zero
 4

SECTION E
31.(i)
  1. State Lenz's law and explain how this law is a consequence of conservation of energy principle.
  2. A square shaped loop of side {tex}\frac{l}{2}{/tex} is initially lying outside a region of uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} as shown in the figure. The loop is moved towards right with a constant velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} till it goes out of the region of magnetic field.
    1. What will be the directions of induced current when the loop enters the field and when it leaves the field?
    2. Draw the plots showing the variation of magnetic flux {tex}\phi{/tex} linked with the loop with time {tex}t{/tex} and variation of induced emf E with time t. Mark the relevant values of {tex}{E}, \phi{/tex} and t on the graphs.
 5

OR
(ii)
  1. Differentiate between peak and rms values of alternating current. How are they related?
  2. A current element X is connected across an ac source of emf {tex}{V}={V}_{0} \sin 2 \pi \nu {t}{/tex}. It is found that the voltage leads the current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} radian. If element X was replaced by element Y, the voltage lags behind the current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} radian.
    1. Identify elements X and Y by drawing phasor diagrams.
    2. Obtain the condition of resonance when both elements X and Y are connected in series to the source and obtain expression for resonant frequency. What is the impedance value in this case?
 5
32.(i)
  1. An object is placed 30 cm from a thin convex lens of focal length 10 cm. The lens forms a sharp image on a screen. If a thin concave lens is placed in contact with the convex lens, the sharp image on the screen is formed when the screen is moved by 45 cm from its initial position. Calculate the focal length of the concave lens.
  2. Calculate the angle of minimum deviation of an equilateral prism. The refractive index of the prism is {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}. Calculate the angle of incidence for this case of minimum deviation also.
 5

OR
(ii)
  1. A physics teacher wants to demonstrate interference with the help of double slit experiment using a laser beam of 633 nm wavelength. Since the hall is large enough, interference pattern is formed on the wall 5.0 m from the slits. For clear and comfortable view by all the students they want the fringe width 5 mm.
    1. Find the slit separation for obtaining the desired interference pattern.
    2. How far will the first minimum be from the central maximum?
  2. A parallel beam of light of wavelength 650 nm passes through a slit of width 0.6 mm. The diffraction pattern is obtained on a screen kept 60 cm away from the slit. Find the distance between first order minima on both sides of the central maximum.
 5
33.(i)
  1. Two point charges +q and -q are held at (a, 0) and (-a, 0) in x-y plane. Obtain an expression for the net electric field due to the charges at a point ({tex}0, {y}{/tex} ). Hence, find electric field at a far off point ({tex}{y} \gg {a}{/tex}).
  2. Three point charges of {tex}-2 {nC},-1 {nC}{/tex}, and +5 nC are kept at the vertices {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C of an equilateral triangle of side 0.2 m. Find the total amount of work done in shifting the charges from A to {tex}{A}_{1}, {~B}{/tex} to {tex}{B}_{1}{/tex} and C to {tex}{C}_{1}{/tex}. Here {tex}{A}_{1}, {~B}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{C}_{1}{/tex} are the midpoints of sides {tex}{AB}, {BC}{/tex} and CA, respectively.
 5

OR
(ii)
  1. Show that Gauss's theorem is consistent with Coulomb's law. Using it, derive an expression for the electric field due to a uniformly charged thin spherical shell of radius r at a point at a distance {tex}y{/tex} from the centre of the shell such that (I) {tex}{y} \gt {r}{/tex}, and (II) {tex}{y} \lt {r}{/tex}.
  2. A point charge of +2 nC is kept at the origin of a three-dimensional coordinate system. Find the type and magnitude of the charge which should be kept at ({tex}0,0,-6 {~m}{/tex}) so that the potential due to the system becomes zero at ({tex}0,0,2 {~m}{/tex}).
 5

Explain the following observations using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

  1. Photoelectric emission does not occur from a surface when the frequency of the light incident on it is less than a certain minimum value.
  2. It is the frequency, and not the intensity of the incident light which affects the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons.

    {tex}{h} v={h} v_{o}+{K}_{\text {max }}{/tex}

    {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}={h}\left(v-v_{o}\right){/tex}
    Hence {tex}{K}_{\text {max }} \alpha v{/tex}

  3. {tex}{eV}_{{o}}={h} \nu-{h} \nu_{{o}}{/tex}
    {tex}{V}_{{o}}=\frac{{h}}{{e}} v-\frac{{h}}{{e}} v_{{o}}{/tex}

  1. What are majority and minority charge carriers of p-type and n-type semiconductors?
  2. Explain briefly the formation of diffusion current and drift current in a p-n junction diode.
Solution:
  1. In p-type semiconductor
    Majority charge carriers - holes
    Minority charge carriers - electrons
    In n-type semiconductors
    Majority charge carriers - electrons
    Minority charge carriers - holes
  2. Diffusion current - during the formation of p n junction, and due to the concentration gradient across {tex}p{/tex} and {tex}n-{/tex} sides, holes diffuse from {tex}p{/tex} side to {tex}n{/tex} side {tex}(p \rightarrow n){/tex} and electrons diffuse from {tex}n-{/tex} side to {tex}p-{/tex} side {tex}(n \rightarrow p){/tex}. This motion of charge carriers gives rise to diffusion current across the junction.
    Drift current - Due to electric field at junction, an electron on p - side of the junction moves to n - side and a hole on n - side of the junction moves to p side. This motion of charge carriers due to electric field gives drift current.

OR
(ii)
  1. A physics teacher wants to demonstrate interference with the help of double slit experiment using a laser beam of 633 nm wavelength. Since the hall is large enough, interference pattern is formed on the wall 5.0 m from the slits. For clear and comfortable view by all the students they want the fringe width 5 mm.
    1. Find the slit separation for obtaining the desired interference pattern.
    2. How far will the first minimum be from the central maximum?
  2. A parallel beam of light of wavelength 650 nm passes through a slit of width 0.6 mm. The diffraction pattern is obtained on a screen kept 60 cm away from the slit. Find the distance between first order minima on both sides of the central maximum.
Solution:
    1. Slit separation
      {tex} \beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d} {/tex}  
      {tex} {d}=\frac{{D} \lambda}{\beta} {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{633 \times 10^{-9} \times 5}{5 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex}
      {tex} =633 \times 10^{-6} {~m} {/tex}
      {tex} =633 \mu {~m} {/tex}
    2. Distance of first minimum from central maximum
      {tex} {x}_{{n}}=\frac{(2 {n}-1) \lambda {D}}{2 {~d}} {/tex}
      {tex} {n}=1 {/tex}
      {tex} {x}=\frac{633 \times 10^{-9} \times 5}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex}
      {tex} {x}=316.5 \times 10^{-6} {~m} {/tex}
      {tex} {x}=316.5 \mu {~m} {/tex}
  2. Distance between first order minima on both the side
    {tex} {W}=\frac{2 {D} \lambda}{{~d}} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{2 \times 650 \times 10^{-9}}{0.6 \times 10^{-3}} \times 60 \times 10^{-2} {/tex}
    {tex} =1.3 \times 10^{-3} {~m} {/tex}
 5
33.(i)
  1. Two point charges +q and -q are held at (a, 0) and (-a, 0) in x-y plane. Obtain an expression for the net electric field due to the charges at a point ({tex}0, {y}{/tex} ). Hence, find electric field at a far off point ({tex}{y} \gg {a}{/tex}).
  2. Three point charges of {tex}-2 {nC},-1 {nC}{/tex}, and +5 nC are kept at the vertices {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C of an equilateral triangle of side 0.2 m. Find the total amount of work done in shifting the charges from A to {tex}{A}_{1}, {~B}{/tex} to {tex}{B}_{1}{/tex} and C to {tex}{C}_{1}{/tex}. Here {tex}{A}_{1}, {~B}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{C}_{1}{/tex} are the midpoints of sides {tex}{AB}, {BC}{/tex} and CA, respectively.
Solution:

  1. Magnitude of electric field due to the two charges +q and -q are given by
    {tex}{E}_{+{q}}=\frac{{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{1}{{y}^{2}+{a}^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}{E}_{-{q}}=\frac{{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{1}{{y}^{2}+{a}^{2}}{/tex}
    Components normal to the dipole axis cancel out.
    The components along the dipole axis add up.
    The total electric field is opposite to the dipole moment will be given by-
    {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}=-\left({E}_{+{q}}+{E}_{-{q}}\right) \cos \theta \hat{{p}}{/tex}
    {tex}=-\frac{2 {qa}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}\left({y}^{2}+{a}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{{p}} {/tex} {tex}(\hat{{p}}{/tex} is a unit vector along dipole moment{tex}){/tex}
    At large distance ({tex}{y} \gg {a}{/tex})
    {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}=\frac{-2 {qa}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}} y^{3}} \hat{{p}}{/tex}

  2. Initial electrostatic potential energy of the system
    {tex}{U}_{1}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}}\left(\frac{{q}_{{A}} {q}_{{B}}}{{AB}}+\frac{{q}_{{C}} {q}_{{A}}}{{AC}}+\frac{{q}_{C} {q}_{{B}}}{{BC}}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^{9}}{0.2}[(-2 \times-1)+(-2 \times 5){/tex}{tex}+(-1 \times 5)] \times 10^{-18}{/tex}
    {tex}{U}_{1}=-5.85 \times 10^{-7} {~J}{/tex}
    {tex}{U}_{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}}\left(\frac{{q}_{{A}_{1}} {q}_{{B}_{1}}}{{~A}_{1} {~B}_{1}}+\frac{{q}_{{C}_{1}} {q}_{{A}_{1}}}{{~A}_{1} {C}_{1}}+\frac{{q}_{{c}_{1}} {q}_{{B}_{1}}}{{~B}_{1} {C}_{1}}\right){/tex}
    {tex}{U}_{2}=-11.7 \times 10^{-7} {~J}{/tex}
    {tex}{W}={U}_{2}-{U}_{1}=-5.85 \times 10^{-7} {~J}{/tex}
 5

OR
(ii)
  1. Show that Gauss's theorem is consistent with Coulomb's law. Using it, derive an expression for the electric field due to a uniformly charged thin spherical shell of radius r at a point at a distance {tex}y{/tex} from the centre of the shell such that (I) {tex}{y} \gt {r}{/tex}, and (II) {tex}{y} \lt {r}{/tex}.
  2. A point charge of +2 nC is kept at the origin of a three-dimensional coordinate system. Find the type and magnitude of the charge which should be kept at ({tex}0,0,-6 {~m}{/tex}) so that the potential due to the system becomes zero at ({tex}0,0,2 {~m}{/tex}).
Solution:
  1. According to Gauss's theorem
    {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{{E}} . {d} \overrightarrow{{~s}}=\frac{{q}}{\varepsilon_{{o}}}{/tex}
    {tex}{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{{q}}{{r}^{2}}{/tex}
    According to Coulomb's law, force on charge {tex}{q}_{{o}}{/tex} in this field
    {tex}{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{{o}}} \frac{{qq}_{{o}}}{{r}^{2}}{/tex}
    Therefore, Gauss's law is consistent with Coulomb's law

    1. Electric flux through Gaussian surface {tex}{E} \times 4 \pi {y}^{2}{/tex}
      The charge enclosed by the surface {tex}\sigma \times 4 \pi {r}^{2}{/tex}
      Using Gauss theorem
      {tex}{E}\left(4 \pi {y}^{2}\right)=\frac{\sigma 4 \pi {r}^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
      {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}=\frac{{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {y}^{2}} \hat{r}{/tex}
    2. For y < r

      The charge enclosed by Gaussian surface {tex}=0{/tex}
      Using Gauss theorem
      Electric flux {tex}={E}\left(4 \pi {y}^{2}\right)=0{/tex}
      i.e. {tex}E=0 (y \lt r){/tex}

  2. Let the charge is kept at A be q
    Potential at point B due to charge at the origin O and charge (q) at A
    {tex}{V}={V}_{1}+{V}_{2}{/tex}
    {tex}{V}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{2 \times 10^{-9}}{2}+\frac{{q}}{6+2}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[10^{-9}+\frac{{q}}{8}\right]=0{/tex}
    {tex}{q}=-8 \times 10^{-9} {C}{/tex}

In an intrinsic semiconductor, carrier's concentration is {tex}5 \times 10^{8} {~m}^{-3}{/tex} . On doping with impurity atoms, the hole concentration becomes {tex}8 \times 10^{12} {~m}^{-3}{/tex} . Identify (i) the type of dopant and (ii) the extrinsic semiconductor so formed. Calculate the electron concentration in the extrinsic semiconductor. Find the minimum distance of the point from the central maximum at which bright fringes of the interference patterns of the two wavelengths coincide. Solution: {tex}n \lambda_{1}=(n+1) \lambda_{2}{/tex} {tex}n \times 600=(n+1) \times 400{/tex} {tex}\therefore n=2{/tex} {tex}\therefore x=\frac{2 \lambda_{1} D}{d}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}x=1.2 \times 10^{-3} {~m}{/tex} 2 19. Find the focal length of plano-convex lens of refractive index 1.5 and radius of curvature 10 cm when it is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.25. Solution: {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\left(\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{R}_{2}}\right]{/tex} For plano convex lens {tex}{R}_{1}={R}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{2}=\infty{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\left(\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}-1\right) \frac{1}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1.5}{1.25}-1\right) \times \frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{50}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {f}=50 {~cm}{/tex} 2 20. Find the ratio of minimum to maximum wavelength of radiations emitted when electron jumps from higher energy state into ground state of hydrogen atom. Solution: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}={R}\left[\frac{1}{{n}_{{f}}^{2}}-\frac{1}{{n}_{{i}}^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {max }}}={R}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\text {max }}=\frac{4}{3 {R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {min }}}={R}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\text {min }}=\frac{1}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}\left\lvert\, \frac{\lambda_{\text {min }}}{\lambda_{\text {max }}}=\frac{3}{4}\right.{/tex} 2 21. (i) In the given figure, three identical bulbs {tex}{P}, {Q}{/tex} and S are connected to a battery. Compare the brightness of bulbs P and Q with that of bulb S when key K is closed. Compare the brightness of the bulbs S and Q when the key K is opened. Justify your answer in both cases. Solution: Brightness of the bulb 'S' will be more than bulbs 'P' and 'Q' The current flowing through the bulb 'S' is twice of the current in bulbs 'P' and 'Q'. Brightness of the bulb 'S' and 'Q' will be same The current flowing through both bulbs is same. 2 OR (ii) Two cells of emf 10 V each, two resistors of {tex}20 \Omega{/tex} and {tex}10 \Omega{/tex} and a bulb B of {tex}10 \Omega{/tex} resistance are connected together as shown in the figure. Find the current that flows through the bulb. Solution: By applying Kirchhoff's loop rule to closed loops ABCFA and FCDEF {tex}2 {I}_{1}-3 {I}_{2}=1{/tex} ...(i) {tex}{I}_{1}+{I}_{2}=1{/tex} ...(ii) On solving, Current through the bulb, {tex}{I}_{2}=\frac{1}{5} {~A}{/tex} 2 SECTION C 22. Explain the following observations using Einstein's photoelectric equation: Photoelectric emission does not occur from a surface when the frequency of the light incident on it is less than a certain minimum value. It is the frequency, and not the intensity of the incident light which affects the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons. The cut-off voltage ( {tex}{V}_{{o}}{/tex} ) versus frequency ( {tex}v{/tex} ) of the incident light curve is a straight line with a slope {tex}\frac{{h}}{{e}}{/tex} . Solution: Einstein Photo electric equation {tex}{h} v={h} v_{o}+{K}_{\text {max }}{/tex} {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}={h}\left(v-v_{o}\right){/tex} For {tex}v \lt v_{{o}}, K_{\text {max }}{/tex} will be negative Hence, Photoelectric emission is not possible. According to Einstein Photoelectric equation {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}={h}\left(v-v_{o}\right){/tex} Hence {tex}{K}_{\text {max }} \alpha v{/tex} It shows {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}{/tex} depends upon frequency only and not depends upon intensity {tex}{eV}_{{o}}={h} \nu-{h} \nu_{{o}}{/tex} {tex}{V}_{{o}}=\frac{{h}}{{e}} v-\frac{{h}}{{e}} v_{{o}}{/tex} This equation represents the equation of straight line ( {tex}y=m x+c{/tex} ) with the slope {tex}\frac{h}{e}{/tex} . 3 23. A charged particle {tex}q{/tex} moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} is subjected to a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} acting perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow{{v}}{/tex} . If a uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is also set up in the region along the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} , describe the path followed by the particle and draw its shape. How will the magnetic field inside a long solenoid be affected when: the radius of the turns of the solenoid is increased, the length of solenoid as well as the total number of its turns are doubled? Solution: Due to magnetic field particle will follow circular path and due to electric field, particle will accelerate along the electric field. As a result particle will follow a helical path with constant radius but increasing pitch. {tex}{B}=\frac{\mu_{{o}} {NI}}{l}{/tex} No change {tex}\frac{{N}}{l}=\frac{2 {~N}}{2 l}{/tex} No Change 3 24. Differentiate between magnetic flux through an area and magnetic field at a point. A bar magnet is held with its length along the axis of a closed coil. Initially the south pole of the magnet faces the coil. If the magnet is moved towards the coil, explain how a current is induced in the coil and in what direction. Solution: Magnetic flux is equal to total number of magnetic field lines passing normal to given area. Magnetic field at a point in the space around the magnet or moving charge where magnetic force can be experienced. When south pole of the bar magnet moves closer to coil, the magnetic flux through the coil increases. Hence according to Faraday's law induced emf/current generate in the coil. According to Lenz's law near end of the coil become south pole and as a consequence, current flows in the coil is clockwise direction. 3 25. State any three characteristics of electromagnetic waves. Briefly explain how and where the displacement current exists during the charging of a capacitor. Solution: Electromagnetic wave carries energy. Electromagnetic wave carries momentum. Electromagnetic wave moves with velocity of light in vacuum. In electromagnetic wave, electric field vector, magnetic field vector and direction of propagation, all are mutually perpendicular. Electromagnetic waves are transverse in nature. Electromagnetic waves do not require a physical medium to propagate and can travel through a vacuum. Electromagnetic waves consist of oscillating electric and magnetic fields. During charging of capacitor, time varying electric field/electric flux between the plates of capacitor induces the displacement current. Displacement current exists between the plates of capacitor. 3 26. Define 'wavefront' of a light wave. A plane wavefront is refracted from a convex lens. Draw the shape of the refracted wavefront. A plane wave travelling in a medium is incident on a plane surface separating this medium from a rarer medium. Draw a diagram to show refraction of the wave. Hence, verify Snell's law. Solution: Wave front- It is a continuous locus at every point on it, all the particles of medium are vibrating in same phase. Considering triangle ABC and AEC {tex}\operatorname{Sin} {i}=\frac{{BC}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{1} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\operatorname{Sin} r=\frac{A E}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{2} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\frac{\operatorname{Sin} {i}}{\operatorname{Sin} {r}}=\frac{{v}_{1}}{{v}_{2}}=\frac{{n}_{2}}{{n}_{1}}{/tex} {tex}{n}_{1} \operatorname{Sin} {i}={n}_{2} \operatorname{Sin} {r}{/tex} 3 27. What are majority and minority charge carriers of p-type and n-type semiconductors? Explain briefly the formation of diffusion current and drift current in a p-n junction diode. Solution: In p-type semiconductor Majority charge carriers - holes Minority charge carriers - electrons In n-type semiconductors Majority charge carriers - electrons Minority charge carriers - holes Diffusion current - during the formation of p n junction, and due to the concentration gradient across {tex}p{/tex} and {tex}n-{/tex} sides, holes diffuse from {tex}p{/tex} side to {tex}n{/tex} side {tex}(p \rightarrow n){/tex} and electrons diffuse from {tex}n-{/tex} side to {tex}p-{/tex} side {tex}(n \rightarrow p){/tex} . This motion of charge carriers gives rise to diffusion current across the junction. Drift current - Due to electric field at junction, an electron on p - side of the junction moves to n - side and a hole on n - side of the junction moves to p side. This motion of charge carriers due to electric field gives drift current. 3 28. (i) Derive an expression for the resistivity of a conductor in terms of number density of free electrons and relaxation time.
